name: Markkula, Gustav
recent_1: Lee, Y. M., Madigan, R., Wang, Y., Garcia, J., Qin, H., Srinivasan, A., Markkula, G., et al. (2026). Crossing in the dark: Investigating the effect of vehicle kinematics and eHMI on older pedestrians' crossing behavior in a virtual reality experiment. Journal of Safety Research, 96, 315-328. DOI: 10.1016/j.jsr.2026.01.012
recent_1_synopsis: Using a virtual reality experiment, this study examined how vehicle deceleration behavior and external Human-Machine Interfaces (eHMIs) shape older versus younger pedestrians' road-crossing decisions in low-visibility (dark) conditions. Older pedestrians adopted a distinct crossing strategy, compensating for slower crossing speeds by initiating earlier, and they interpreted and used eHMI cues differently than younger pedestrians. The findings matter for designing automated-vehicle communication that is safe and inclusive across age groups.
recent_2: Ma, S., Yan, X., Ma, L., Billington, J., Merat, N., & Markkula, G. (2026). Modelling cognitive load using drift-diffusion models in pedestrian street-crossing: a method supported by neural evidence. Accident Analysis & Prevention, 225, 108310. DOI: 10.1016/j.aap.2025.108310
recent_2_synopsis: This work used drift-diffusion (evidence-accumulation) models to quantify how cognitive load alters pedestrians' street-crossing decisions, validating model predictions against the centro-parietal positive potential (CPP), a neural signature of evidence accumulation. Cognitive load reduced sensitivity to time-to-arrival, slowed responses, and raised collision risk, with the best model showing lowered responsiveness to decision utilities under load. It demonstrates that neurally grounded computational models can capture how secondary-task demand degrades safety-critical pedestrian behavior.
recent_3: Kalantari, A. H., Lin, Y.-S., Mohammadi, A., Merat, N., & Markkula, G. (2025). Testing the validity of multiparticipant distributed simulation for understanding and modeling road user interaction. IEEE Transactions on Human-Machine Systems, 55(5). DOI: 10.1109/THMS.2025.3591506
recent_3_synopsis: This paper validates distributed (networked) driving simulation, in which a human driver and a human pedestrian interact simultaneously in a shared virtual environment, as a method for studying genuine road-user interaction. By comparing behavior across kinematic conditions, the authors assess whether multiparticipant simulation reproduces realistic interactive dynamics that conventional single-participant setups cannot. The work supports a new experimental paradigm for collecting interaction data to build and test models of vehicle-pedestrian negotiation.
seminal_1: Engstrom, J., Markkula, G., Victor, T., & Merat, N. (2017). Effects of cognitive load on driving performance: The cognitive control hypothesis. Human Factors, 59(5), 734-764. DOI: 10.1177/0018720817690639
seminal_1_synopsis: This highly cited paper (approximately 250 cites per the seed list; ~382 cites on Google Scholar as of June 2026) proposes the 'cognitive control hypothesis' to reconcile conflicting findings on how cognitive load affects driving. It argues that cognitive load selectively impairs performance on tasks requiring cognitive control while leaving (or even improving) automatized, routine driving behaviors intact. The framework is foundational for understanding driver distraction and has guided subsequent research on secondary-task interference and attention in driving.
seminal_2: McDonald, A. D., Alambeigi, H., Engstrom, J., Markkula, G., Vogelpohl, T., Dunne, J., & Yuma, N. (2019). Toward computational simulations of behavior during automated driving takeovers: A review of the empirical and modeling literatures. Human Factors, 61(4), 642-688. DOI: 10.1177/0018720819829572
seminal_2_synopsis: This review (approximately 249 cites per the seed list; ~365 cites on Google Scholar as of June 2026) synthesizes the empirical and modeling literature on how drivers resume control when automated driving systems hand back the driving task. It maps the determinants of takeover performance and lays out a roadmap toward computational simulation of takeover behavior. It is foundational for human-factors research on conditionally automated driving and the design of safe transition-of-control protocols.
expertise_overview: Gustav Markkula specializes in quantitative, cognitive computational modeling of road-user behavior and interaction. His core methods include evidence-accumulation (drift-diffusion) models, sensorimotor and neuromuscular control models of steering and braking, and increasingly machine-learning and multi-agent reinforcement-learning approaches, often validated against neural signals (e.g., the centro-parietal positive potential) and naturalistic or simulator data. Application domains center on driver behavior (distraction, cognitive load, emergency braking, takeover from automation) and vulnerable road users, especially pedestrian crossing decisions and driver-pedestrian negotiation. He works extensively with driving and distributed (multiparticipant) virtual-reality simulation, external Human-Machine Interface (eHMI) communication, and gap-acceptance and merging interaction modeling. A recurring theme is building mechanistic, theory-grounded models of human perception, decision-making, and action that can support the virtual testing and safe design of automated vehicles.
notes: Identification confidence: HIGH. Confirmed as Gustav Markkula, Chair in Applied Behaviour Modelling at the Institute for Transport Studies, University of Leeds (computational driver/road-user modeling, automated driving). Identity confirmed via University of Leeds faculty page (environment.leeds.ac.uk/transport/staff/957), Google Scholar profile (user 55G7VxoAAAAJ), and dblp; all match the pre-verified Human Factors DOIs. Citation counts: seed-list counts used as primary; Google Scholar counts (total ~6,733 cites, h-index 46) retrieved June 2026 and noted as higher (382 and 365 for the two seminal papers). DOI verification via Crossref (June 2026): recent_1 10.1016/j.jsr.2026.01.012 VERIFIED (title + Journal of Safety Research, 2026-02); recent_2 10.1016/j.aap.2025.108310 VERIFIED (Accident Analysis & Prevention, 2026-02); recent_3 10.1109/THMS.2025.3591506 VERIFIED (IEEE Transactions on Human-Machine Systems, 2025-10); seminal_1 10.1177/0018720817690639 and seminal_2 10.1177/0018720819829572 are pre-verified Human Factors DOIs from the seed list. No unverifiable citations included. Note: recent_3 volume/issue (55(5)) inferred from THMS 2025 numbering and not independently confirmed; the DOI itself is verified. Author lists for recent_1 are truncated with 'et al.' (large multi-author paper).
